overallVerdict: Aggressively inflated. The GitHub tells a different story than the pitch.

claims.summary: React activity spans 7 months, not 3 years. Earliest React commit dates to April 2024.
claims.evidence: Oldest React repo created April 2024. No React code before that. Total React LOC across all repos: 2,847 — one semester of weekend projects, not three years.

receipts.label: Oldest React repo
receipts.detail: react-dashboard created April 14, 2024 — 7 months ago, not 3 years

receipts.label: Total React LOC
receipts.detail: 2,847 lines across 3 repos. Consistent with ~6 months of part-time work.

claims.whatToAskNext: Ask them to walk through a React performance issue they debugged — specifically why they chose their state management approach and what they'd do differently now.

claims.summary: Found an ML-adjacent repo but no evidence of production deployment. It's a Jupyter notebook with hardcoded sample data.
claims.evidence: ml-pipeline-demo contains 1 notebook, 340 lines, sample CSV hardcoded as input, no deployment config, no CI, no requirements.txt pinned versions. README claims 'serves predictions in real-time' — no server code found.

receipts.label: README claim vs reality
receipts.detail: README: 'production ML pipeline serving real-time predictions.' Repo contents: 1 Jupyter notebook, 1 CSV file, no API, no server.

receipts.label: No deployment config
receipts.detail: No Dockerfile, no CI/CD, no requirements.txt, no .env.example. Nothing that suggests this ever ran outside a laptop.

receipts.label: Suspiciously linear history
receipts.detail: 14 commits, all in a single 6-hour window on Nov 3. No iteration, no debugging, no reverts.

claims.whatToAskNext: Ask them to describe the infrastructure — where it ran, how predictions were served, what the latency was, how they monitored it. If it was production, they'll have instant answers.

claims.claim: Led backend architecture for a real-time system
claims.summary: Found the repo. Candidate authored 9% of commits. All contributions were in a single 3-day window after the core architecture was already in place.
claims.evidence: distributed-auth-service: 247 commits total. Candidate: 22 commits. Core architecture — WebSocket layer, Redis pub/sub, connection pooling — committed by @nico-dev across weeks 1–4. Candidate joined in week 5, contributed UI glue code and README.

receipts.label: Commit attribution
receipts.detail: 22 of 247 commits (9%). Architecture commits: 0. Contributions: README, minor config changes, one bugfix.

receipts.label: Architecture already done
receipts.detail: WebSocket layer implemented by @nico-dev on Oct 1. Candidate's first commit: Oct 29 — 'update readme and fix typo'

receipts.label: Core architecture files
receipts.detail: websocket.ts, redis-pubsub.ts, connection-pool.ts — all authored by @nico-dev, zero commits from candidate on these files.

claims.whatToAskNext: Ask them to draw the architecture from memory — specifically the connection pooling strategy and why they chose Redis pub/sub over alternatives. Ask what they'd change if rebuilding it today.

redemption: The React Dashboard has genuinely thoughtful component composition for someone 7 months in. The custom hook pattern in useDataFetcher.ts shows real understanding, not just tutorial copying. There's a developer here — just not the one described.
